does: This paper develops PairSAE, a mechanistic interpretability method that extracts interpretable features from protein co-folding foundation models like Boltz-2. The approach addresses a fundamental challenge in applying sparse autoencoders to pairformer architectures by using N-mode SVD to compress pairwise interaction tensors into token-wise roles, then learning shared token-level features via sparse autoencoders that decode into both sequence and pair representations simultaneously. Evaluated on Boltz-2 activations from PLINDER protein-ligand complexes, PairSAE discovers interpretable features that correlate with UniProt functional annotations and predict affinity scores, demonstrating that the method can surface meaningful biological concepts embedded in the model's latent space.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature applying sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to mechanistic interpretability of neural networks, extending techniques previously developed for transformer sequence embeddings to the more complex setting of pairformer architectures dominant in structural biology. It responds to a specific technical limitation: standard SAEs fail cleanly on pairwise representations because naive application creates quadratic feature blow-up and scatters interpretable concepts across both sequence and pair spaces. The paper implicitly connects to prior work on understanding foundation models in biology and the broader challenge of interpretability in large neural networks, while engaging with the practical need to understand what drives predictions in AlphaFold-style models increasingly used for protein design.
enables: PairSAE opens avenues for systematic mechanistic understanding of protein language models and co-folding architectures, potentially enabling researchers to verify that models learn sensible biological relationships before deploying them for protein design. The method could support validation of foundation models for structural biology by ensuring learned representations align with known biology. Future work might extend the approach to other pairformer-based models beyond Boltz-2, develop automated methods to discover and name learned features, or integrate interpretability findings into design pipelines by identifying which internal mechanisms drive desired structural outcomes. This interpretability infrastructure could help catch failure modes or unexpected behaviors in structure prediction models used for therapeutic protein design.
